comments:
Summary
This paper presents an approach to automatic stroke generation for oriental ink painting (Sumi-e) by modeling a soft-tuft brush as a reinforcement learning (RL) agent. The authors define a continuous-state, continuous-action MDP, design a state representation relative to local shape features (medial axis, curvature, offset), propose an immediate reward encouraging smooth, well-placed footprints, and train a Gaussian policy with a policy-gradient algorithm. Training is performed on local partial shapes extracted from a small stroke library, and the learned policy is applied to unseen shapes and photo-to-ink conversions. The paper compares the RL approach to a prior dynamic programming (DP) method and reports better returns and much lower computation time.

Strengths
- Clear motivation: learning a policy generalizable to new shapes addresses limitations of shape-specific DP solutions.
- Careful design of state features that are local and relative to the medial axis is sensible and likely important for generalization.
- Use of a continuous policy gradient method is appropriate for obtaining smooth trajectories in continuous action/state spaces.
- Empirical results show qualitative success on unseen shapes and quantitative comparison against a DP baseline (tradeoff between return and computation time).
- Practical application (photo conversion) demonstrates potential usefulness.

Weaknesses and issues
- Limited quantitative evaluation and ablation: The paper reports average return and computation time and shows qualitative images, but there is little analysis of (i) sensitivity to hyperparameters (λ, τ, ζ, α, σ initialization), (ii) importance of individual state features (which components of s are most critical), or (iii) behavior under different training set sizes. An ablation study would strengthen claims about the contribution of the state/reward design.
- Training data and generalization: The training library contains 80 strokes from which local patches are sampled. It is unclear how diverse these are and whether the learned policy generalizes to very different shapes (e.g., very jagged contours or highly varying scale) beyond the shown examples.
- Baseline comparison could be more comprehensive/fair: The DP method performance depends on discretization (number of candidates). Although the authors sweep this parameter, additional baselines (e.g., simple heuristics, other stroke extraction methods) or a perceptual user study would provide a stronger assessment of visual quality. It is also not fully clear that the DP setup reported is the best practical configuration; more discussion on fairness of comparison is desirable.
- Reward design and stability: The reward has the form 1 + (curvature term)/(λ1 E_location + λ2 E_posture). This inverse relationship can produce large rewards if the denominator is small; the paper does not discuss numerical stability or normalization across shapes. The variance reduction baseline b is introduced but practical implementation details (how b is estimated online, if at all) are not fully described.
- Policy parameterization is simple (linear mean µ>s and scalar std σ). This limits expressiveness; the paper does not justify why linear policies suffice or explore richer parameterizations (e.g., nonlinear features or neural networks). More discussion of limitations would help.
- Some implementation details are omitted or vague: e.g., how Actions 2–4 (scaling, rotation) are ‘‘automatically optimized’’ when Action 1 is primary; exact transition dynamics and whether the simulator is deterministic/noisy; how episodes are terminated in practice.
- No user/perceptual evaluation: Given that the task is aesthetic, a small human study or blind comparison would strengthen claims about ‘‘natural’’ or ‘‘graceful’’ strokes.

Suggestions for improvement
- Add ablation experiments: remove or alter individual state features (ω, φ, d, κ1, κ2, l) and report effect on performance and qualitative results. Show sensitivity to α, λ, τ, ζ choices.
- Provide more implementation details: how the baseline b is computed, how Actions 2–4 are selected/optimized, termination criteria, and transition noise (if any).
- Evaluate generalization more widely: test on shapes with different scales, branchings, or highly concave boundaries; report failure cases.
- Consider comparing to additional baselines (simple heuristics, other stroke-based rendering approaches) and include a small user study assessing perceived quality.
- Discuss limitations of the linear Gaussian policy and whether more expressive policies would help.
- Release code/data or provide enough pseudo-code to enable reproducibility.

Recommendation rationale
The paper addresses a well-defined problem with a reasonable methodological choice (policy gradient on a carefully designed local state). The contributions are mostly engineering and application-driven (state/reward/training-session design) rather than introducing fundamentally new RL algorithms, but the application is novel and the empirical results are promising. Given the moderate novelty, solid technical execution, and practical utility, I recommend acceptance with revisions that add more evaluation and clarification.
